Search the FormalConjectures codebase for any existing definitions or utilities related to:
1. Group counting / number of groups of order n / group enumeration
2. MulEquiv, GroupStructIso, or similar group isomorphism definitions
3. Quotient of Group structures / counting group structures
4. Any OEIS A000001 references
5. Any other Erdős problems that define similar counting functions (numGroupsOfOrder or similar)

Be thorough - check both FormalConjectures/ and any Util/ directories. Report all findings with file paths and line numbers.

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1160
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1160](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1160)
    23→
    24→If $g(n)$ denotes the number of groups of order $n$, then $g(n) \le g(2^m)$ for all $n \le 2^m$.
    25→
    26→[Va99] Vaughan, R.C., _Problems in combinatorial number theory_, 1999.
    27→
    28→[BNV07] Blackburn, S.R., Neumann, P.M. and Venkataraman, G., _Enumeration of finite groups_,
    29→Cambridge Tracts in Mathematics, 2007.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Classical
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos1160
    35→
    36→/-- Two group structures on the same type are isomorphic if there exists
    37→a multiplicative equivalence between them. -/
    38→def GroupStructIso (n : ℕ) (G₁ G₂ : Group (Fin n)) : Prop :=
    39→  Nonempty (@MulEquiv (Fin n) (Fin n) G₁.toMul G₂.toMul)
    40→
    41→/-- Group isomorphism is an equivalence relation on group structures on $\operatorname{Fin}(n)$. -/
    42→instance groupStructSetoid (n : ℕ) : Setoid (Group (Fin n)) where
    43→  r := GroupStructIso n
    44→  iseqv := by
    45→    refine ⟨?_, ?_, ?_⟩
    46→    · intro G
    47→      exact ⟨@MulEquiv.refl (Fin n) G.toMul⟩
    48→    · intro G₁ G₂ ⟨e⟩
    49→      exact ⟨@MulEquiv.symm (Fin n) (Fin n) G₁.toMul G₂.toMul e⟩
    50→    · intro G₁ G₂ G₃ ⟨e₁⟩ ⟨e₂⟩
    51→      exact ⟨@MulEquiv.trans (Fin n) (Fin n) (Fin n) G₁.toMul G₂.toMul G₃.toMul e₁ e₂⟩
    52→
    53→/-- The number of isomorphism classes of groups of order $n$ (OEIS A000001).
    54→Defined as the cardinality of group structures on $\operatorname{Fin}(n)$ modulo isomorphism. -/
    55→noncomputable def numGroupsOfOrder (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    56→  Nat.card (Quotient (groupStructSetoid n))
    57→
    58→/--
    59→Erdős Problem 1160 [Va99, 5.71]:
    60→
    61→Let $g(n)$ denote the number of groups of order $n$. If $n \le 2^m$ then $g(n) \le g(2^m)$.
    62→
    63→This conjecture states that among all $n \le 2^m$, the value $g(n)$ is maximized
    64→at $n = 2^m$ (i.e., powers of 2 have the most groups of any order up to that
    65→point). Listed as Question 22.16 in [BNV07], attributed to Erdős and Higman.
    66→-/
    67→@[category research open, AMS 20]
    68→theorem erdos_1160 (n m : ℕ) (h : n ≤ 2 ^ m) :
    69→    numGroupsOfOrder n ≤ numGroupsOfOrder (2 ^ m) := by
    70→  sorry
    71→
    72→end Erdos1160
    73→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1161
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1161](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1161)
    23→
    24→Let $f_k(n)$ count the number of elements of $S_n$ (the symmetric group) of
    25→order $k$. For which values of $k$ will $f_k(n)$ be maximal?
    26→
    27→Beker [Be25d] proved that
    28→$$\max_{k \geq 1} f_k(n) \sim (n-1)!,$$
    29→and characterized the maximizing values of $k$: for all large $n$,
    30→$f_k(n) = (n-1)!$ if and only if $k \geq 1$ is minimal such that
    31→$\operatorname{lcm}(1,\ldots,n-k) \mid k$.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open Finset Equiv
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos1161
    37→
    38→/-- $f_k(n)$: the number of permutations in $S_n$ whose order equals $k$. -/
    39→noncomputable def countPermsOfOrder (n k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    40→  ((Finset.univ : Finset (Equiv.Perm (Fin n))).filter (fun σ => orderOf σ = k)).card
    41→
    42→/--
    43→Erdős Problem 1161 (Solved by Beker [Be25d]):
    44→
    45→Let $f_k(n)$ count the number of elements of $S_n$ of order $k$. Beker proved that
    46→$\max_{k \geq 1} f_k(n) \sim (n-1)!$, i.e., the maximum over $k$ of the number of
    47→permutations of order $k$ is asymptotic to $(n-1)!$.
    48→
    49→Formalized as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $n$,
    50→(1) there exists $k \geq 1$ with $f_k(n) \geq (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot (n-1)!$, and
    51→(2) for all $k$, $f_k(n) \leq (1 + \varepsilon) \cdot (n-1)!$.
    52→-/
    53→@[category research solved, AMS 5 20]
    54→theorem erdos_1161 :
    55→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    56→      ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    57→        (∃ k : ℕ, k ≥ 1 ∧
    58→          (countPermsOfOrder n k : ℝ) ≥ (1 - ε) * ((n - 1).factorial : ℝ)) ∧
    59→        (∀ k : ℕ,
    60→          (countPermsOfOrder n k : ℝ) ≤ (1 + ε) * ((n - 1).factorial : ℝ)) := by
    61→  sorry
    62→
    63→end Erdos1161
    64→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1162
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1162](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1162)
    23→
    24→Give an asymptotic formula for the number of subgroups of $S_n$.
    25→Is there a statistical theorem on their order?
    26→
    27→A problem of Erdős and Turán.
    28→
    29→Let $f(n)$ count the number of subgroups of $S_n$.
    30→Pyber [Py93] proved that $\log f(n) \asymp n^2$.
    31→Roney-Dougal and Tracey [RoTr25] proved that $\log f(n) = (\tfrac{1}{16} + o(1))n^2$.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open Filter Real
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos1162
    37→
    38→/-- The number of subgroups of the symmetric group $S_n$. -/
    39→noncomputable def numSubgroups (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    40→  Nat.card (Subgroup (Equiv.Perm (Fin n)))
    41→
    42→/--
    43→Erdős Problem 1162 (partially resolved by Roney-Dougal and Tracey [RoTr25]):
    44→
    45→$\log f(n) / n^2 \to \tfrac{1}{16}$ as $n \to \infty$, where $f(n)$ is the number of
    46→subgroups of $S_n$.
    47→
    48→The full problem, asking for an asymptotic formula for $f(n)$ and a statistical
    49→theorem on the orders of subgroups, remains open.
    50→-/
    51→@[category research solved, AMS 20]
    52→theorem erdos_1162 :
    53→    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => Real.log (numSubgroups n : ℝ) / ((n : ℝ) ^ 2))
    54→      atTop (nhds (1 / 16)) := by
    55→  sorry
    56→
    57→end Erdos1162
    58→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1163
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1163](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1163)
    23→
    24→Describe (by statistical means) the arithmetic structure of the orders of
    25→subgroups of $S_n$. A concrete interpretation: the proportion of divisors of
    26→$n!$ that are orders of subgroups of $S_n$ tends to $0$ as $n \to \infty$.
    27→
    28→[Va99] Vardi, I., *Computational Recreations in Mathematica* (1999).
    29→-/
    30→
    31→open Equiv Classical
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos1163
    34→
    35→/-- A natural number $m$ is a subgroup order of $S_n$ if there exists a subgroup
    36→of the symmetric group `Perm (Fin n)` with exactly $m$ elements. -/
    37→def IsSubgroupOrderOfSn (n m : ℕ) : Prop :=
    38→  ∃ H : Subgroup (Perm (Fin n)), Nat.card H = m
    39→
    40→/--
    41→Erdős Problem 1163 (Erdős and Turán) [Va99, 5.74]:
    42→Describe (by statistical means) the arithmetic structure of the orders of
    43→subgroups of $S_n$.
    44→
    45→The original problem is acknowledged as ambiguous. A concrete
    46→interpretation: as $n \to \infty$, the proportion of divisors of $n!$ that are orders
    47→of subgroups of $S_n$ tends to $0$. By Lagrange's theorem, every subgroup order
    48→divides $n!$, but the set of subgroup orders becomes a vanishingly small
    49→fraction of all divisors.
    50→-/
    51→@[category research open, AMS 5 20]
    52→theorem erdos_1163 :
    53→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    54→      ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n ≥ N,
    55→        ((n.factorial.divisors.filter (fun m => IsSubgroupOrderOfSn n m)).card : ℝ) <
    56→        ε * (n.factorial.divisors.card : ℝ) := by
    57→  sorry
    58→
    59→end Erdos1163
    60→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1179
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1179](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1179)
    23→
    24→Let $0 < \varepsilon < 1$ and let $g_\varepsilon(N)$ be the minimal $k$ such that if $G$ is an
    25→abelian group of size $N$ and $A \subseteq G$ is a uniformly random subset of size $k$, and
    26→$$F_A(g) = \#\{S \subseteq A : g = \sum_{x \in S} x\},$$
    27→then, with probability $\to 1$ as $N \to \infty$,
    28→$$|F_A(g) - 2^k/N| \leq \varepsilon \cdot 2^k/N$$
    29→for all $g \in G$. Estimate $g_\varepsilon(N)$; in particular, is it true that for all
    30→$\varepsilon > 0$,
    31→$$g_\varepsilon(N) = (1 + o_\varepsilon(1)) \log_2 N?$$
    32→
    33→It is trivial that $g_\varepsilon(N) \geq \log_2 N$. Erdős and Rényi proved
    34→$g_\varepsilon(N) \leq (2 + o(1)) \log_2 N + O_\varepsilon(1)$, and Erdős and Hall improved
    35→this to
    36→$$g_\varepsilon(N) \leq (1 + O_\varepsilon(\log \log \log N / \log \log N)) \log_2 N.$$
    37→
    38→A problem of Erdős.
    39→-/
    40→
    41→open Filter
    42→
    43→open scoped Topology Real
    44→
    45→namespace Erdos1179
    46→
    47→/-- For a finite abelian group $G$, a subset $A \subseteq G$, and element $g \in G$,
    48→$F_A(g) = \#\{S \subseteq A : \sum_{x \in S} x = g\}$ counts the subsets of $A$ (including
    49→$\emptyset$) whose element-sum equals $g$. -/
    50→def subsetSumCount {G : Type*} [AddCommGroup G] [DecidableEq G]
    51→    (A : Finset G) (g : G) : ℕ :=
    52→  (A.powerset.filter (fun S => S.sum id = g)).card
    53→
    54→/-- A $k$-subset $A$ of a finite abelian group $G$ of size $N$ is
    55→$\varepsilon$-approximation-uniform if the subset-sum counts are uniformly close to their
    56→"expected" value $2^k/N$:
    57→$$|F_A(g) - 2^k/N| \leq \varepsilon \cdot 2^k/N \quad \text{for all } g \in G.$$ -/
    58→def IsApproxUniform {G : Type*} [AddCommGroup G] [Fintype G] [DecidableEq G]
    59→    (ε : ℝ) (A : Finset G) : Prop :=
    60→  let N : ℕ := Fintype.card G
    61→  let k : ℕ := A.card
    62→  ∀ g : G, |((subsetSumCount A g : ℝ) - (2 : ℝ) ^ k / N)| ≤ ε * (2 : ℝ) ^ k / N
    63→
    64→/-- The fraction of $k$-subsets $A$ of a finite abelian group $G$ that are
    65→$\varepsilon$-approximation-uniform. This models the probability that a uniformly random
    66→$k$-subset satisfies the approximation. -/
    67→noncomputable def goodFraction {G : Type*} [AddCommGroup G] [Fintype G] [DecidableEq G]
    68→    (ε : ℝ) (k : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    69→  let all := (Finset.univ : Finset G).powersetCard k
    70→  let good := @Finset.filter (Finset G) (fun A => IsApproxUniform ε A) (Classical.decPred _) all
    71→  good.card / all.card
    72→
    73→/-- $g_\varepsilon(N)$ is the minimal $k$ such that for every finite abelian group $G$ of order
    74→$N$, the fraction of $k$-subsets that are $\varepsilon$-approximation-uniform is at least
    75→$1 - 1/N$. This captures the condition "with probability $\to 1$ as $N \to \infty$" for
    76→each $N$. -/
    77→noncomputable def gEps (ε : ℝ) (N : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    78→  sInf {k : ℕ | ∀ (G : Type) [hG : AddCommGroup G] [hF : Fintype G] [hD : DecidableEq G],
    79→    Fintype.card G = N →
    80→    @goodFraction G hG hF hD ε k ≥ 1 - 1 / (N : ℝ)}
    81→
    82→/--
    83→Erdős Problem 1179:
    84→
    85→For all $0 < \varepsilon < 1$, $g_\varepsilon(N) = (1 + o_\varepsilon(1)) \log_2 N$ as
    86→$N \to \infty$, i.e., $g_\varepsilon(N) / \log_2 N \to 1$ as $N \to \infty$.
    87→
    88→Here $g_\varepsilon(N)$ is the minimal $k$ such that for every abelian group $G$ of order $N$,
    89→at least a $(1 - 1/N)$ fraction of all $k$-subsets $A$ of $G$ satisfy
    90→$$|F_A(g) - 2^k/N| \leq \varepsilon \cdot 2^k/N \quad \text{for all } g \in G,$$
    91→where $F_A(g) = \#\{S \subseteq A : \sum_{x \in S} x = g\}$.
    92→
    93→The lower bound $g_\varepsilon(N) \geq \log_2 N$ is trivial.
    94→Erdős and Rényi proved $g_\varepsilon(N) \leq (2 + o(1)) \log_2 N + O_\varepsilon(1)$.
    95→Erdős and Hall proved
    96→$g_\varepsilon(N) \leq (1 + O_\varepsilon(\log \log \log N / \log \log N)) \log_2 N$.
    97→-/
    98→@[category research open, AMS 5 60]
    99→theorem erdos_1179 : answer(sorry) ↔
   100→    ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε → ε < 1 →
   101→    Tendsto (fun N : ℕ => (gEps ε N : ℝ) / Real.logb 2 N)
   102→      atTop (nhds 1) := by
   103→  sorry
   104→
   105→end Erdos1179
   106→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1149
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1149](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1149)
    23→
    24→[Va99] Vardi, I., *Computational Recreations in Mathematica* (1999), Problem 1.34.
    25→
    26→[BeRi17] Bergelson, V. and Richter, F. K., *Dynamical generalizations of the prime
    27→number theorem and disjointness of additive and multiplicative semigroup actions* (2017).
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open Finset Filter Real Classical
    31→
    32→open scoped Topology
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos1149
    35→
    36→/--
    37→Erdős Problem 1149 [Va99, 1.34]:
    38→
    39→Let $\alpha > 0$ be a real number, not an integer. The natural density of integers
    40→$n \geq 1$ for which $\gcd(n, \lfloor n^\alpha \rfloor) = 1$ equals $6/\pi^2$.
    41→
    42→The constant $6/\pi^2 = 1/\zeta(2)$ is the "probability" that two random integers
    43→are coprime, so this says $n$ and $\lfloor n^\alpha \rfloor$ behave like independent
    44→random integers with respect to coprimality when $\alpha$ is not an integer.
    45→
    46→This was proved by Bergelson and Richter [BeRi17].
    47→-/
    48→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    49→theorem erdos_1149 (α : ℝ) (hα_pos : 0 < α) (hα_not_int : ∀ k : ℤ, (k : ℝ) ≠ α) :
    50→    Tendsto (fun x : ℕ =>
    51→      (((Finset.Icc 1 x).filter (fun n =>
    52→        Nat.Coprime n (⌊(n : ℝ) ^ α⌋₊))).card : ℝ) / (x : ℝ))
    53→      atTop (nhds (6 / Real.pi ^ 2)) := by
    54→  sorry
    55→
    56→end Erdos1149
    57→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Leinster Groups
    21→
    22→A finite group is a Leinster group if the sum of the orders of all its normal subgroups
    23→equals twice the group's order.
    24→
    25→*References:*
    26→* [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leinster_group)
    27→* Leinster, Tom (2001). "Perfect numbers and groups".
    28→  [arXiv:math/0104012](https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0104012)
    29→
    30→TODO: The following properties from the Wikipedia article can also be formalized:
    31→- There are no Leinster groups that are symmetric or alternating.
    32→- There is no Leinster group of order p²q² where p, q are primes.
    33→- No finite semi-simple group is Leinster.
    34→- No p-group can be a Leinster group.
    35→- All abelian Leinster groups are cyclic with order equal to a perfect number.
    36→-/
    37→
    38→namespace LeinsterGroup
    39→
    40→open scoped Classical
    41→
    42→/--
    43→A finite group `G` is a **Leinster group** if the sum of the orders of all its normal subgroups
    44→equals twice the group's order.
    45→-/
    46→def IsLeinster (G : Type*) [Group G] [Fintype G] : Prop :=
    47→  ∑ H : {H : Subgroup G // H.Normal}, Nat.card H = 2 * Fintype.card G
    48→
    49→/--
    50→**Conjecture:** Are there infinitely many Leinster groups?
    51→
    52→This asks whether there exist infinitely many (non-isomorphic) finite groups that are
    53→Leinster groups.
    54→
    55→Formalized via the negation of "Does there exist an n such that all Leinster groups have
    56→order less than n".
    57→-/
    58→@[category research open, AMS 20]
    59→theorem infinitely_many_leinster_groups : answer(sorry) ↔
    60→    ¬∃ n : ℕ, ∀ G : Type, ∀ (_ : Group G) (_ : Fintype G),
    61→      IsLeinster G → Fintype.card G < n := by
    62→  sorry
    63→
    64→/--
    65→Cyclic groups of perfect number order are Leinster groups.
    66→
    67→This follows from the fact that for a cyclic group, all subgroups are normal and correspond
    68→to divisors of the group order, and a number is perfect if and only if the sum of its divisors
    69→(including itself) equals twice the number.
    70→-/
    71→@[category API, AMS 20]
    72→theorem cyclic_of_perfect_is_leinster (G : Type*) [Group G] [Fintype G]
    73→    [IsCyclic G] (h_perfect : Nat.Perfect (Fintype.card G)) :
    74→    IsLeinster G := by
    75→  sorry
    76→
    77→/--
    78→An abelian group is a Leinster group if and only if it is cyclic with order equal
    79→to a perfect number.
    80→
    81→Reference: Leinster, Tom (2001). "Perfect numbers and groups". Theorem 2.1.
    82→-/
    83→@[category research solved, AMS 20]
    84→theorem abelian_is_leinster_iff_cyclic_perfect (G : Type*) [CommGroup G] [Fintype G] :
    85→    IsLeinster G ↔ IsCyclic G ∧ Nat.Perfect (Fintype.card G) := by
    86→  sorry
    87→
    88→/--
    89→Non-abelian Leinster groups exist.
    90→
    91→For example, `S₃ × C₅` (order 30) and `A₅ × C₁₅₁₂₈` are Leinster groups.
    92→
    93→Reference: Leinster, Tom (2001). "Perfect numbers and groups".
    94→-/
    95→@[category research solved, AMS 20]
    96→theorem exists_nonabelian_leinster_group :
    97→    ∃ G : Type, ∃ (_ : Group G) (_ : Fintype G),
    98→      IsLeinster G ∧ ¬ ∀ (a b : G), a * b = b * a := by
    99→  sorry
   100→
   101→/--
   102→The dihedral group `DihedralGroup n` (of order `2n`) is a Leinster group if and only if `n` is
   103→an odd perfect number. This gives a one-to-one correspondence between dihedral Leinster groups
   104→and odd perfect numbers.
   105→
   106→In particular, the existence of odd perfect numbers is equivalent to the existence of
   107→dihedral Leinster groups.
   108→
   109→Reference: Leinster, Tom (2001). "Perfect numbers and groups".
   110→-/
   111→@[category research solved, AMS 20]
   112→theorem dihedral_is_leinster_iff_odd_perfect (n : ℕ) [NeZero n] :
   113→    IsLeinster (DihedralGroup n) ↔ Nat.Perfect n ∧ Odd n := by
   114→  sorry
   115→
   116→end LeinsterGroup
   117→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 638
    21→
    22→Is it true that for every infinite cardinal $\kappa$ there is a graph $G$ such that every
    23→finite induced subgraph of $G$ belongs to a given family $S$ with the Ramsey property, and
    24→every $\kappa$-colouring of the edges of $G$ contains a monochromatic triangle?
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/638](https://www.erdosproblems.com/638)
    27→-/
    28→
    29→open SimpleGraph Cardinal
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos638
    32→
    33→/-- A symmetric colouring of a simple graph has a **monochromatic triangle**
    34→    if three pairwise-adjacent vertices have all three edges the same colour. -/
    35→def HasMonoTriangle {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) {α : Type*}
    36→    (c : V → V → α) : Prop :=
    37→  ∃ a b d : V, G.Adj a b ∧ G.Adj b d ∧ G.Adj a d ∧
    38→    c a b = c b d ∧ c a b = c a d
    39→
    40→/-- The induced subgraph of $G$ pulled back along an embedding
    41→    $f : \operatorname{Fin} m \hookrightarrow V$. -/
    42→def inducedFinSubgraph {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) {m : ℕ}
    43→    (f : Fin m ↪ V) : SimpleGraph (Fin m) where
    44→  Adj i j := G.Adj (f i) (f j)
    45→  symm _ _ h := G.symm h
    46→  loopless i h := G.loopless (f i) h
    47→
    48→/-- Two graphs on $\operatorname{Fin} m$ are isomorphic via a permutation of vertices. -/
    49→def FinGraphIso {m : ℕ} (G H : SimpleGraph (Fin m)) : Prop :=
    50→  ∃ σ : Equiv.Perm (Fin m), ∀ i j, G.Adj i j ↔ H.Adj (σ i) (σ j)
    51→
    52→/--
    53→**Erdős Problem 638**
    54→
    55→Let $S$ be a family of finite graphs (indexed by vertex count) with the Ramsey
    56→property: for every $n \geq 1$, some member of $S$ forces a monochromatic triangle
    57→under any $n$-colouring of its edges.
    58→
    59→Is it true that for every infinite cardinal $\kappa$, there exists a graph $G$ such that
    60→every finite induced subgraph of $G$ belongs (up to isomorphism) to $S$, and
    61→every $\kappa$-colouring of the edges of $G$ contains a monochromatic triangle?
    62→-/
    63→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    64→theorem erdos_638 : answer(sorry) ↔
    65→    ∀ (S : (m : ℕ) → Set (SimpleGraph (Fin m)))
    66→      (_ : ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ 1 →
    67→        ∃ m : ℕ, ∃ G ∈ S m,
    68→          ∀ (c : Fin m → Fin m → Fin n), (∀ u v, c u v = c v u) →
    69→            HasMonoTriangle G c)
    70→      (κ : Cardinal) (_ : ℵ₀ ≤ κ),
    71→    ∃ (V : Type) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    72→      (∀ (m : ℕ) (f : Fin m ↪ V),
    73→        ∃ H ∈ S m, FinGraphIso (inducedFinSubgraph G f) H) ∧
    74→      ∀ (α : Type) (_ : #α = κ) (c : V → V → α),
    75→        (∀ u v, c u v = c v u) → HasMonoTriangle G c := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos638
    79→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 426
    21→
    22→Let $f(n)$ be the maximum number of unique subgraphs in a graph on $n$ vertices.
    23→Bradač and Christoph proved that $f(n) = o(2^{\binom{n}{2}} / n!)$.
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/426](https://www.erdosproblems.com/426)
    26→
    27→[EnEr72] Erdős, P. and Entringer, R. C. (1972).
    28→
    29→[Br75] Brouwer, A. E. (1975).
    30→
    31→[BrCh24] Bradač, D. and Christoph, M. (2024).
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open SimpleGraph
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos426
    37→
    38→/--
    39→Two subgraphs of a graph $G$ are isomorphic if there exists a graph isomorphism
    40→between their coerced graphs (viewed as simple graphs on their respective
    41→vertex subtypes).
    42→-/
    43→def SubgraphIsomorphic {V : Type*} {G : SimpleGraph V}
    44→    (H₁ H₂ : G.Subgraph) : Prop :=
    45→  Nonempty (H₁.coe ≃g H₂.coe)
    46→
    47→/--
    48→A subgraph $H$ of $G$ is a "unique subgraph" if it is the only subgraph of $G$
    49→in its isomorphism class: every subgraph $H'$ of $G$ isomorphic to $H$ must equal $H$.
    50→-/
    51→def IsUniqueSubgraph {V : Type*} {G : SimpleGraph V}
    52→    (H : G.Subgraph) : Prop :=
    53→  ∀ H' : G.Subgraph, SubgraphIsomorphic H H' → H' = H
    54→
    55→/--
    56→The number of unique subgraphs of a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices.
    57→-/
    58→noncomputable def numUniqueSubgraphs {n : ℕ} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) : ℕ :=
    59→  Set.ncard {H : G.Subgraph | IsUniqueSubgraph H}
    60→
    61→/--
    62→$f(n)$ is the maximum number of unique subgraphs over all graphs on $n$ vertices.
    63→-/
    64→noncomputable def maxUniqueSubgraphs (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    65→  sSup {k : ℕ | ∃ G : SimpleGraph (Fin n), numUniqueSubgraphs G = k}
    66→
    67→/--
    68→Erdős Problem 426 (Disproved):
    69→
    70→We say $H$ is a unique subgraph of $G$ if there is exactly one way to find $H$ as a
    71→subgraph (not necessarily induced) of $G$. Let $f(n)$ be the maximum number of
    72→distinct unique subgraphs in a graph on $n$ vertices.
    73→
    74→A problem of Erdős and Entringer [EnEr72], who constructed a graph with
    75→$\gg 2^{\binom{n}{2} - O(n^{3/2+o(1)})}$ many unique subgraphs. This was improved by
    76→Brouwer [Br75], who constructed a graph with $\gg 2^{\binom{n}{2} - O(n)} / n!$ many
    77→unique subgraphs.
    78→
    79→Note that there are approximately $2^{\binom{n}{2}} / n!$ non-isomorphic graphs on $n$
    80→vertices, so the bound in the problem statement is trivially best possible.
    81→
    82→Erdős believed Brouwer's construction was essentially best possible, but
    83→Spencer suggested that $\gg 2^{\binom{n}{2}} / n!$ might be achievable. Erdős offered
    84→100 dollars for such a construction and 25 dollars for a disproof.
    85→
    86→Bradač and Christoph [BrCh24] proved the answer is no:
    87→$$f(n) = o(2^{\binom{n}{2}} / n!).$$
    88→Quantitatively, the $o(1)$ factor can be taken to be $O(\log \log \log n / \log \log n)$.
    89→-/
    90→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    91→theorem erdos_426 :
    92→    Filter.Tendsto
    93→      (fun n : ℕ =>
    94→        (maxUniqueSubgraphs n : ℝ) /
    95→        ((2 : ℝ) ^ (n.choose 2) / (Nat.factorial n : ℝ)))
    96→      Filter.atTop
    97→      (nhds 0) := by
    98→  sorry
    99→
   100→end Erdos426

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 117
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/117](https://www.erdosproblems.com/117)
    23→
    24→Let $h(n)$ be the least $k$ such that every group where each subset of more than $n$ elements
    25→contains two distinct commuting elements can be covered by $k$ abelian subgroups. Pyber proved
    26→$c_1^n < h(n) < c_2^n$ for constants $c_2 > c_1 > 1$.
    27→
    28→[Er90] Erdős, P., *Some of my favourite unsolved problems*. A tribute to Paul Erdős (1990),
    29→467-478.
    30→
    31→[Er97f] Erdős, P., *Some of my favourite problems which recently have been solved*. Proceedings
    32→of the International Conference on Discrete Mathematics (ICDM) (1997).
    33→
    34→[Va99] Vadász, V., *On the commuting properties of finite groups* (1999).
    35→
    36→[Py87] Pyber, L., *The number of pairwise non-commuting elements and the index of the centre in
    37→a finite group*. J. London Math. Soc. (2) 35 (1987), 287-295.
    38→-/
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos117
    41→
    42→/-- A group $G$ satisfies the $n$-commuting property if every finite subset of size
    43→greater than $n$ contains two distinct elements $x \neq y$ with $xy = yx$. -/
    44→def HasNCommutingProperty (n : ℕ) (G : Type*) [Group G] : Prop :=
    45→  ∀ (S : Finset G), n < S.card →
    46→    ∃ x ∈ S, ∃ y ∈ S, x ≠ y ∧ x * y = y * x
    47→
    48→/-- A group $G$ can be covered by at most $k$ Abelian subgroups: there exist $k$ subgroups
    49→$H_0, \ldots, H_{k-1}$ (possibly with repetition), each of which is abelian, whose union
    50→is all of $G$. -/
    51→def CoveredByAbelianSubgroups (k : ℕ) (G : Type*) [Group G] : Prop :=
    52→  ∃ (H : Fin k → Subgroup G),
    53→    (∀ i, ∀ a b : G, a ∈ H i → b ∈ H i → a * b = b * a) ∧
    54→    ∀ g : G, ∃ i, g ∈ H i
    55→
    56→/-- $h(n)$ is the least $k$ such that every group satisfying the $n$-commuting property
    57→can be covered by at most $k$ Abelian subgroups. -/
    58→noncomputable def erdosH (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    59→  sInf {k : ℕ | ∀ (G : Type) [Group G],
    60→    HasNCommutingProperty n G → CoveredByAbelianSubgroups k G}
    61→
    62→/--
    63→Erdős Problem 117 [Er90, Er97f, Va99]:
    64→
    65→Let $h(n)$ be minimal such that any group $G$ satisfying the property that every
    66→subset of more than $n$ elements contains distinct commuting elements $x \neq y$
    67→($xy = yx$) can be covered by at most $h(n)$ Abelian subgroups.
    68→
    69→Estimate $h(n)$ as well as possible.
    70→
    71→Pyber [Py87] proved there exist constants $c_2 > c_1 > 1$ such that
    72→$$c_1^n < h(n) < c_2^n.$$
    73→The lower bound $c_1^n < h(n)$ was already known to Isaacs [Er97f].
    74→The precise exponential growth rate of $h(n)$ remains open.
    75→-/
    76→@[category research solved, AMS 20]
    77→theorem erdos_117 :
    78→    ∃ c₁ c₂ : ℝ, 1 < c₁ ∧ c₁ < c₂ ∧
    79→    ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n →
    80→      c₁ ^ n < (erdosH n : ℝ) ∧ (erdosH n : ℝ) < c₂ ^ n := by
    81→  sorry
    82→
    83→end Erdos117
    84→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 274
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→[erdosproblems.com/274](https://www.erdosproblems.com/274)
    24→[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Herzog%E2%80%93Sch%C3%B6nheim_conjecture)
    25→-/
    26→
    27→open scoped Pointwise Cardinal
    28→
    29→namespace Erdos274
    30→
    31→-- TODO(callesonne): add already proved results from the wiki page
    32→
    33→/-- An exact covering of a group `G` is a finite collection of subgroups `{H_1, ..., H_k}` and
    34→representative `{g_1, ..., g_k}` such that the cosets `g_iH_i` are pairwise disjoint and their
    35→union covers `G`.
    36→
    37→Note that this differs from `Partition (α := Subgroup G)` because the covering condition there
    38→invokes `Subgroup.sup` which is subgroup generation and thus stronger than union. This definition
    39→is easier to use in this contect than the alternative `Partition (α := Set G)`, which lacks
    40→subgroup definitions such as `Subgroup.index`. -/
    41→structure Group.ExactCovering (G : Type*) [Group G] (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
    42→  parts : ι → Subgroup G
    43→  reps : ι → G
    44→  nonempty (i : ι) : (parts i : Set G).Nonempty
    45→  disjoint : (Set.univ (α := ι)).PairwiseDisjoint fun (i : ι) ↦ reps i • (parts i : Set G)
    46→  covers : ⋃ i, reps i • (parts i : Set G) = Set.univ
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Does there exist a group `G` with an exact covering by more than one cosets of
    50→different sizes? (i.e. each element is contained in exactly one of the cosets.)
    51→-/
    52→@[category research open, AMS 20]
    53→theorem erdos_274 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ (G : Type*) (h : Group G) (hG : 1 < ENat.card G)
    54→    (ι : Type*) (_ : Fintype ι) (P : Group.ExactCovering G ι),
    55→      1 < Fintype.card ι ∧ (Set.range P.parts).Pairwise fun A B ↦ #A ≠ #B := by
